title: Battle of Agincourt
tagline: 6,000 English. 36,000 French. The mud chose a side.
hook_line: Henry V has 6,000 sick, starving English soldiers. The French have 36,000 armored knights. The French knights are so confident they're gambling on who gets to ransom Henry personally. They have not thought about the mud.

scenes.title: What the French Saw
scenes.narration: October 25, 1415. The Constable of France looked at the English army across the field at Agincourt and did not feel afraid. He felt impatient. 6,000 English soldiers — cold, sick with dysentery, outnumbered six to one, retreating toward Calais for three weeks. Against them: 36,000 French knights in full plate armor, the finest cavalry in Europe, the cream of French nobility. The Constable's problem was not the battle. His problem was that too many knights wanted to be in the front row.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic wide establishing shot of Agincourt battlefield, October morning, gray overcast sky — left side: enormous French army, 36,000 soldiers, gleaming plate armor catching dull light, dense formations stretching across the frame, French heraldic banners (blue with gold fleurs-de-lis), mounted knights in the foreground — right side: the small English force, ragged lines, fewer men visible, longbowmen with stakes in the ground in front of them, Henry V's banner — the contrast in size genuinely overwhelming, the French side feels inevitable, #2C1810 and steel gray and French blue, cinematic epic scale

scenes.title: 36,000 Knights
scenes.narration: The French army at Agincourt was not merely large. It was the assembled flower of French chivalry — dukes, counts, marshals, the Constable of France himself. They had so many knights that three full divisions stood in reserve and still outnumbered the entire English force. French knights had been gambling the night before on who would capture King Henry personally. The ransom would make a man rich for life. Several French noblemen had already composed accounts of the coming victory to send home. The battle had not yet begun.
scenes.visual_prompt: French command camp the night before — French nobles in opulent campaign tents, candlelight, maps on tables, but the atmosphere is celebratory rather than strategic — noblemen visible in animated discussion (gambling), their attendants polishing already immaculate armor, a scribe visible writing a letter (the pre-victory accounts mentioned), heraldic crests everywhere — the sheer wealth and power on display communicating genuine dominance, the English numbers visible on a casualty-estimate scroll showing 6,000 — someone has crossed it out and written 'irrelevant', cinematic #2C1810 and warm candlelight amber

scenes.title: Henry's Army
scenes.narration: Henry V's army was sick. The three-week retreat had given dysentery to a significant portion of his force. English soldiers had cut the seats out of their breeches for practicality. They were cold, outnumbered, and in enemy territory. Henry walked among his men the night before the battle — alone, without guards, in a borrowed cloak. He talked to them. The night before Agincourt is what Shakespeare wrote his Saint Crispin's Day speech about. Whether Henry said those exact words, no one knows. That he walked among them in the dark, nobody disputes.
scenes.visual_prompt: Night scene in the English camp, October darkness, campfires small and sparse compared to the blaze of French fires visible in the far background — Henry V in a plain brown traveling cloak over his armor (unidentifiable as the king), moving between small groups of soldiers, sitting briefly with some, standing with others — his soldiers visible: sick but not broken, longbows carefully maintained, faces showing the particular exhaustion of people who have decided they're going to do this anyway — cinematic night lighting, firelight, #2C1810 and warm embers, contrast with French opulence

scenes.title: The Longbow Problem — For the French
scenes.narration: The English longbow was the most lethal weapon on any medieval battlefield. A trained English longbowman could fire twelve aimed shots per minute. At Agincourt, Henry had 5,000 longbowmen. That is 60,000 arrows per minute. At a range of 250 meters, a longbow arrow will penetrate plate armor. French knights in full plate weighed 250 to 300 pounds with their horse. The French plan was to charge the English line. The charge covered 250 meters. The French plan was made by people who had not done the arithmetic on arrows.
scenes.visual_prompt: Technical visualization of the longbow problem — infographic style but cinematic: showing an English longbowman and the rate of fire (12 arrows per minute marked), then multiplied by 5,000 archers (60,000 arrows per minute), then the 250-meter gap to the French lines marked, then an arrow penetrating a plate armor diagram — and finally the charge path for a 250-300 pound armored knight on an armored horse needing to cover those 250 meters — the math communicated visually and brutally, #2C1810 and #C9A227 detail work, the lethal efficiency clear

scenes.title: The Mud
scenes.narration: It had rained heavily the night before. The newly plowed field between the two armies was thick mud. When the French knights began their charge — and they did charge, eventually, after hours of standoff — their armored horses immediately began to slow. Horses in mud with a 300-pound load do not gallop. They struggle. The charge that should have taken 40 seconds took three minutes. 5,000 English longbowmen had three minutes. The arrows darkened the sky.
scenes.visual_prompt: The charge begins — dramatic slow-motion composition: French knights in gleaming plate beginning the charge, horses at full gallop at the start — then the mud: horses' hooves sinking, the charge slowing, armored knights beginning to crowd together as the terrain compresses the French formation — overhead arrow volley visible filling the sky, the arrows coming down in waves — French knights beginning to fall, some horses going down in the mud, the momentum of the charge failing in real time — cinematic epic scale, the mud as the decisive factor, #2C1810 and steel gray, #C9A227 arrow shafts filling the air

scenes.title: When the Knights Fell
scenes.narration: French knights who survived the arrows reached the English line already exhausted, mud-covered, separated from their mounts. They faced English longbowmen who had planted stakes and now drew short swords and mallets. A French knight in full plate on his feet in thick mud, exhausted from 250 meters of struggle, against an English soldier with a mallet and a knife — the armor meant nothing. The English killed through visors, through gaps, through the mud that had pulled the knights down. The field became so full of French dead and fallen that the second and third waves had nowhere to advance. They were stopped not by soldiers but by the bodies of their own army.
scenes.visual_prompt: The brutal close-quarters battle aftermath — English longbowmen with short mallets and knives working through fallen armored French knights, the field showing the scale of French casualties, armored knights face-down in mud, some trying to rise and unable under the weight of their armor, the English outnumbered but standing while the French are grounded — the composition showing the reversal: French armor that should have been invincible made irrelevant by mud and exhaustion — cinematic battle, not glorified but honest, #2C1810 and gray, the mud the dominant color of the field

scenes.title: The Count
scenes.narration: When it was over: 6,000 English soldiers with 112 dead. 36,000 French soldiers with between 7,000 and 10,000 dead, including the Constable of France, three dukes, five counts, and ninety barons. The flower of French nobility had died in a muddy field in northern France. Among the French dead: several of the men who had written letters home the night before describing their coming victory.
scenes.visual_prompt: Aftermath panoramic — the field of Agincourt after the battle, the contrast between the small English force still standing and the scale of French casualties visible across the field — French heraldic banners in the mud, the sheer number of fallen armored knights — Henry V in the foreground looking at the field, not triumphant, the weight of it visible — a letter visible on the ground near a fallen French nobleman, partially unrolled, describing a victory that did not happen — cinematic aftermath lighting, gray sky, #2C1810 and muted tones, respectful gravity

scenes.title: What the Longbow Ended
scenes.narration: Agincourt didn't just defeat a French army. It ended an era. The armored knight on horseback had been the dominant military force in Europe for five hundred years. The longbow — a weapon a peasant could use after years of training — killed that era at Agincourt. The nobility of France had their sons trained in horsemanship, swordsmanship, lance craft, all the arts of mounted warfare. None of it mattered against 5,000 men with bent wood and string who had practiced since age seven.
scenes.visual_prompt: Historical transition visualization — split composition: left shows the 500-year era of the armored knight, images of medieval cavalry dominance from the Crusades through 1400 — right shows the Agincourt aftermath marking the turn, then the progression beyond: the declining role of heavy cavalry in European warfare, the rise of infantry and missile weapons — the armored knight as an icon being crossed out not by a weapon but by a technology available to common soldiers — cinematic historical infographic style, #2C1810 and #C9A227, the weight of a five-century shift

scenes.title: France Does Not Stop
scenes.narration: The defeat at Agincourt did not end French power. It ended French confidence, for a generation. The Hundred Years' War continued for another thirty-eight years. And then, from the village of Domrémy, a seventeen-year-old girl who said she heard the voices of saints came to the Dauphin of France with a claim: she knew how to break the English siege of Orléans. Her name was Joan of Arc. France's answer to Agincourt took thirty-eight years to arrive — and arrived in the form of a teenager with a banner.
scenes.visual_prompt: Powerful two-part composition — left: the ruins of French confidence after Agincourt, the thirty-eight years of continued war mapped — English control of France shown spreading across a map, the scale of what was lost — right: the emergence of Joan of Arc, seventeen years old, in white armor and her banner (a white flag with lilies and the names of Jesus and Mary), her face young and certain, the village of Domrémy small in the background — the contrast between the scale of French defeat and the scale of France's eventual answer, #2C1810 and #C9A227, the weight of history

scenes.title: The Letters That Were Never Sent
scenes.narration: After the battle, English soldiers went through the French camp. They found the letters. Several French noblemen had written detailed accounts of the coming battle — their certain victory, the English king's capture, the terms they planned to offer. The letters were never sent. They were found in a muddy field, in the tents of men who had not survived to send them. History is full of letters written in confidence. History has a poor record of delivering them.
scenes.visual_prompt: Intimate close-up composition — English soldiers going through an abandoned French tent, finding a handful of unsent letters — one shown partially unrolled: ornate handwriting, French script, visible phrases suggesting confidence in victory — the letter muddied, the seal unbroken, the intended recipient never reached — outside the tent through the opening: the aftermath of the field visible — the gap between what the letter describes and what actually happened — cinematic close-up, warm candlelight interior against the gray outside, #2C1810 and warm amber, the irony quiet and deliberate

viral_hook: The French knights wrote letters home describing their coming victory. The letters were found in the mud after the battle. Unsent. Agincourt changed the history of warfare in a plowed field that had rained the night before.
highlight_note: The charge slowing in the mud — showing that 40-second charge becoming three minutes, and what 5,000 longbowmen do with three minutes. The mud as the decisive weapon.
rewatch_bait: Scene 2 — the French nobleman's scribe writing the pre-victory letter. Scene 10 reveals the letter undelivered. The same handwriting, the same letter, different meaning. The scribe in scene 2 is writing his own irony.
youtube_title: 36,000 French knights vs 6,000 English — and the mud won 🌧️ | Gods & Glory S2 EP2
lesson: Confidence in your advantages is not the same as understanding the terrain. The French had more knights. The field had more mud.
arc_notes: GG S2 E02. Villain mandate: French army feels genuinely overwhelming — 36,000 to 6,000, finest knights in Europe, gambling on Henry's ransom. They lose to weather and terrain, not to English superiority. Joan of Arc planted in scene 9 — potential GG future episode. The letter motif (scene 2 and scene 10) is the episode's signature rewatch detail.
